## 8. Key Flow (sequence diagram)

**Which flow and why:** the full lease lifecycle `request → activate → return`, because it touches every class and shows the State pattern and the Strategy pattern both firing at runtime.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    actor Operator
    participant Svc as LeasingService
    participant M as VendingMachine
    participant L as Lease
    participant B as BillingStrategy

    Operator->>Svc: request_lease(VM-1, operator, term)
    Svc->>M: hold("lease-1")
    M-->>Svc: ok (current_lease_id = lease-1)
    Svc-->>Operator: Lease(RESERVED)

    Operator->>Svc: activate_lease("lease-1")
    Svc->>L: activate(now)
    L-->>Svc: status RESERVED -> ACTIVE

    Operator->>Svc: return_lease("lease-1", revenue)
    Svc->>L: mark_returned()
    L-->>Svc: status ACTIVE -> RETURNED
    Svc->>M: release("lease-1")
    Svc->>B: cost_cents(term, revenue)
    B-->>Svc: 120000
    Svc-->>Operator: Lease(RETURNED, final_bill_cents=120000)
```

🎙️ **Arrow by arrow:** "The operator calls `request_lease` on the service. The service doesn't track availability itself — it asks the `VendingMachine` to `hold` the lease; that's the atomic check-and-set. It returns a RESERVED lease. On `activate`, the service delegates to the `Lease`, which guards its own RESERVED→ACTIVE transition — the service never writes the status. On `return`, the lease transitions itself to RETURNED, the machine is released, and here's where Strategy fires: the service calls `cost_cents` on whatever `BillingStrategy` the lease's plan maps to — it has no idea whether that's flat or revenue-share. The closing bill rides back on the lease."

**Why it shows the design is good:** the service coordinates but implements no business logic — every decision delegates to the entity (transition rules) or the strategy (pricing). That's Open/Closed at runtime.

## 9. Concurrency, Thread-Safety, Edge Cases & Extensibility

**DEFAULT: describe the solution. Write code only if the interviewer asks.**

🗣️ **Step 1 — name the race:** "It's in `request_lease`. Two operators call it on the same free machine. Both read `current_lease_id is None`, both proceed, both `hold` it — double-lease. That's a check-then-act race: the check and the act aren't one atomic step."

🗣️ **Step 2 — the solution:** "Give each machine its own lock. Before the check-and-hold, acquire that machine's lock; release after. Both operators race for the lock — one wins and holds the machine, the other waits, then sees it's taken and gets `MachineNotAvailableError`."

🗣️ **Step 3 — the trade-off:** "Per-machine, not one global lock — so leasing VM-1 and VM-2 happens in parallel; only same-machine requests serialize, and that's microseconds. I keep the lock off the read-only listing path."

🗣️ **Step 4 — distributed:** "Across servers an in-process lock is useless — each process has its own memory. I'd push atomicity to the DB with a compare-and-set: `UPDATE machine SET current_lease_id=? WHERE id=? AND current_lease_id IS NULL`. Whoever updates 0 rows lost the race and gets the clean error."

**Concurrency vocabulary**

| Term | Plain meaning | Where it appears here |
|---|---|---|
| Race condition | Two threads touch shared state at once; result depends on timing | two operators in `request_lease` on one machine |
| Critical section | The block that must run as one atomic step | `hold` + register in `request_lease` |
| Check-then-act | Check a condition, then act — state changed in between | "saw `current_lease_id is None`, then set it" |
| Atomic | All-or-nothing; no half-done state observable | what the per-machine lock guarantees |
| Mutex / lock | A token only one thread holds at a time | `RLock` per machine |
| Reentrant (RLock) | Same thread can re-acquire a lock it holds | `return_lease` holds it, calls `compute_bill`, which re-takes it |
| Lock granularity | How much one lock covers — coarse vs fine | per-machine, not one global lock |
| Pessimistic locking | Grab the lock before touching state | the in-process `RLock` |
| Optimistic locking | Don't lock; write conditionally, retry if you lost | the DB compare-and-set for the distributed case |
| Compare-and-set (CAS) | Atomic "update only if value is still X" | `WHERE current_lease_id IS NULL` |
| Deadlock | Two threads each hold a lock the other needs | avoided: only ever ONE lock held at a time |

**Concurrency follow-up Q&A**

> ❓ "Why `RLock`, not a plain `Lock`?"
> "Because a guarded method calls another guarded method on the same machine: `return_lease` holds the machine lock and calls `compute_bill`, which re-acquires it. A plain `Lock` would deadlock on the second acquire by the same thread; an `RLock` lets the owner re-enter and only fully releases when the outermost `with` exits."

> ❓ "Why per-machine and not one global lock?"
> "A global lock serializes the whole fleet — leasing VM-1 needlessly blocks VM-2 though they share nothing. Per-machine locking lets different machines lease in parallel; only operators racing the *same* machine serialize. I also keep `compute_bill`'s notify-free read short so the lock is held for microseconds."

> ❓ "What's the deadlock risk?"
> "None by construction — I only ever hold one lock at a time (the per-machine lock), and the one re-entry (`return_lease`→`compute_bill`) is the same thread re-taking the same lock, which `RLock` handles. No lock-ordering cycle exists."

> ❓ "Pessimistic or optimistic — which and why?"
> "Pessimistic in-process: the critical section is two lines and contention on one machine is low, so a short-held lock beats a retry loop. For the distributed case I flip to optimistic — a DB compare-and-set — because you can't hold a Python lock across servers and a conditional write is the idiomatic atomic there."

> ❓ "Is `list_available` safe under threads?"
> "It's a read-only scan of derived state. It might momentarily include a machine another thread is mid-`hold` on, but the truth is reconfirmed atomically when that operator actually calls `request_lease` — the listing is advisory, the `hold` is authoritative."

> ❓ "What if the lock-holder crashes mid-critical-section?"
> "In-process, a crash takes the whole process down, so there's no orphaned lock to clean up. In the distributed CAS version, the conditional update is a single atomic statement — it either committed or didn't, no partial state."

### ❓ IF ASKED — the thread-safe version

**Part A — the no-op hook + subclass:**
```python
# ─── §6 clean core — no locks anywhere in the file ───
@contextmanager
def _lock(self, machine: VendingMachine) -> Iterator[None]:
    yield                                    # does nothing

# ─── §9 thread-safe subclass — overrides ONLY this method ───
class ThreadSafeLeasingService(LeasingService):
    def __init__(self, billing=None, now_fn=datetime.now) -> None:
        super().__init__(billing=billing, now_fn=now_fn)
        self._locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()    # guards the dict of locks itself

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, machine: VendingMachine) -> Iterator[None]:
        with self._map_lock:                 # lock the MAP first (double-lock idiom)
            lock = self._locks.setdefault(machine.machine_id, threading.RLock())
        with lock:                           # then hold only the per-machine lock
            yield
```
- **Why `RLock` not `Lock`:** `return_lease` (holding the lock) calls `compute_bill` (re-takes it) — same thread, second acquire.
- **Why per-machine not global:** different machines lease in parallel.
- **The double-lock idiom:** lock `_map_lock` to create/fetch the per-machine lock safely, then release it and hold only the machine lock — two threads can't corrupt the dict while creating the same key.

**Part B — the exact modified method:**
```python
# ─── §6 version (clean, single-threaded) ───
def request_lease(self, machine_id: str, operator: Operator, term: BillingTerm) -> Lease:
    machine = self._require_machine(machine_id)
    lease = Lease(lease_id=f"lease-{next(self._lease_seq)}",
                  machine_id=machine_id, operator=operator, term=term)
    with self._lock(machine):                # no-op in the clean core
        machine.hold(lease.lease_id)         # ← race point: check-and-set
        self._leases[lease.lease_id] = lease
    return lease

# ─── §9 version (thread-safe) — diff: ZERO lines in this method ───
# request_lease is UNCHANGED. The only change is that `_lock` now returns a real
# per-machine RLock (the subclass above). The critical section was already wrapped
# in `with self._lock(machine):`, so the same code is now atomic for free.
```
🎙️ "The beauty of the Template Method hook: `request_lease` doesn't change at all between §6 and §9. It already wraps the check-and-hold in `with self._lock(machine)`. In the clean core that's a no-op; in the subclass it's a real per-machine `RLock`. The diff is the subclass's ~10 lines, not the business method."

**Part C — proof (the actual Barrier test + result):**
```python
def test_fifty_operators_same_machine_exactly_one_wins(flat):
    tsvc = ThreadSafeLeasingService()
    tsvc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    wins, losses = _race_for_machine(tsvc, "VM-1", 50, flat)   # Barrier(50) inside
    assert len(wins) == 1                  # exactly one winner
    assert len(losses) == 49               # everyone else rejected
    assert all("already leased" in m for m in losses)
    held = [m for m in tsvc._machines.values() if not m.is_available()]
    assert len(held) == 1                  # one machine held — the unique-resource check
```

25 passed in 0.02s
```
🎙️ "The `Barrier(50)` makes all 50 threads hit `request_lease` at the same instant — without it they'd take turns and there'd be no real race. Exactly one wins, 49 get `MachineNotAvailableError`, and exactly one machine ends up held. The 50-on-5 test adds the unique-resource assertion: five winners, five distinct machines."

**Edge cases handled**

- Unknown machine id → `UnknownMachineError` → `test_unknown_machine_rejected`
- Machine already leased → `MachineNotAvailableError` → `test_request_on_held_machine_rejected`
- Cancel an ACTIVE lease → `IllegalLeaseTransitionError` → `test_cannot_cancel_active_lease`
- Return a RESERVED lease → `IllegalLeaseTransitionError` → `test_cannot_return_reserved_lease`
- Double-activate → `IllegalLeaseTransitionError` → `test_cannot_activate_twice`
- Act on a terminal lease → `IllegalLeaseTransitionError` → `test_cannot_act_on_terminal_lease`
- Cancelled lease bill → returns 0 → `test_cancelled_lease_owes_nothing`
- Invalid term (months/bps) → `InvalidLeaseTermError` → `test_invalid_term_rejected`
- Expiry + idempotent sweep → silent reclaim → `test_sweep_expires_due_active_leases`, `test_sweep_is_idempotent`
- 50 operators / 1 machine → exactly one wins → `test_fifty_operators_same_machine_exactly_one_wins`

### Extensibility — interviewer twists

**(i) Level calibration:** how many of these you get tracks your target level — junior candidates often get none, mid-level one or two, senior several with depth. Prep all three below if you're targeting senior.

**(ii) Summary table**

| Want to add | How (one class/method) | What in the core changes |
|---|---|---|
| A new pricing plan (tiered, usage-based) | new `BillingStrategy` subclass + 1 map entry | nothing |
| Machines that go under maintenance | add `OUT_OF_SERVICE`; `is_available` checks it | one line |
| A grace/RENEWAL state before expiry | add `LeaseStatus.RENEWING` + table edges | the transition table only |
| Lease-event notifications | add an `Observer` seam on the service | one notify call (new seam) |

**(iii) Twist walkthroughs**

> **Twist 1 — "Add a tiered pricing plan that charges more per machine after the 5th."**
>
> Nothing in the core prices anything — it delegates to a strategy.
>
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "That's a new `BillingStrategy` subclass and one entry in the `_billing` map. The service's `compute_bill` doesn't change — it already looks up the strategy by `lease.term.plan`."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> class TieredPricing(BillingStrategy):
>     plan = BillingPlan.TIERED
>     def cost_cents(self, term, reported_revenue_cents):
>         base = term.monthly_rate_cents * term.term_months
>         return base if term.machine_count <= 5 else int(base * 1.25)
> # registry: self._billing[BillingPlan.TIERED] = TieredPricing()
> ```
> **Trade-off:** adds a `machine_count` field to `BillingTerm`; trivial and additive. Worth it the moment a second pricing shape appears.

> **Twist 2 — "Machines can break and go under maintenance — don't lease them."**
>
> Today availability is purely "no lease holds me"; a broken-but-free machine would still be leasable.
>
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "I add an `OUT_OF_SERVICE` flag to the machine and have `is_available` also check it. `request_lease` is unchanged — `hold` already refuses anything not available, and `list_available` filters through the same method."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> # VendingMachine
> in_service: bool = True
> def is_available(self) -> bool:
>     return self.current_lease_id is None and self.in_service
> def hold(self, lease_id):
>     if not self.in_service:
>         raise MachineNotAvailableError(f"{self.machine_id} is out of service")
>     ...                          # existing check-and-set
> ```
> **Trade-off:** one new field + one guard line; the lifecycle is untouched because maintenance is a *machine* concern, not a *lease* state.

> **Twist 3 — "Split activation into a two-phase commit: reserve, then confirm physical install before the term starts."**
>
> Today `request_lease` reserves and `activate_lease` starts the term in one operator action.
>
> 🎙️ **What you say:** "The state machine already supports this — RESERVED is the holding state. I'd add a `PENDING_INSTALL` state between RESERVED and ACTIVE, plus an auto-cancel timeout so a reservation that never installs frees the machine. Two new table edges, one new state."
>
> **The change:**
> ```python
> # LeaseStatus: add PENDING_INSTALL
> _LEGAL_TRANSITIONS = {
>     RESERVED: {PENDING_INSTALL, CANCELLED},
>     PENDING_INSTALL: {ACTIVE, CANCELLED},   # confirm install -> ACTIVE
>     ACTIVE: {RETURNED, EXPIRED},
>     ...
> }
> ```
> **Trade-off:** adds a state + a timeout sweep; worth it in production where you must guarantee physical install before billing, overkill for a single-phase v1.

**Hardest follow-up questions**

> ❓ "Hardest concurrency case for this design?"
> "A `return_lease` racing the expiry `sweep_expired` on the same machine — both want to free it and move the lease terminal. The per-machine `RLock` serializes them; whichever transitions first wins, and the second sees the lease is already terminal (`IllegalLeaseTransitionError`) or `release` no-ops because `current_lease_id` no longer matches. No double-free, no orphan."

> ❓ "What if you have multiple app servers?"
> "An in-process `RLock` only guards one process. I'd move the atomicity to the database: `UPDATE machine SET current_lease_id=:lid WHERE id=:mid AND current_lease_id IS NULL`. The row count tells you who won; 0 rows = lost the race = `MachineNotAvailableError`. The `Lease` state machine logic stays identical — only the `hold` step becomes a conditional write."

> ❓ "What if you have 10M machines and 1M active leases?"
> "The dict registries are fine in memory for that count, but I'd back them with a database and load by id. `list_available` becomes an indexed query (`WHERE current_lease_id IS NULL`) instead of an O(n) scan, and `sweep_expired` becomes a ranged query on `expires_at` with a partial index — never a full-fleet scan."

> ❓ "What if `return_lease` fails halfway — say the closing-bill step throws?"
> "Everything in `return_lease` is in-memory under one lock, so a throw mid-method leaves the lease either fully RETURNED or untouched — there's no external side effect to undo. If billing called an external charge service, I'd split it: transition + release commit locally, then enqueue the charge as a separate idempotent job, so a billing outage never strands the machine."

🎙️ **Final 30-second summary:** "The key insight is that AVAILABLE belongs to the machine, not the lease — so the lease is the only state machine and availability is just the absence of a holding lease. The service coordinates through two seams: a `Lease` State machine that guards its own transitions, and a `BillingStrategy` the service prices through without knowing the plan — new plans are new classes, zero core changes. The one concurrency risk is the check-then-act in `request_lease`; I guard it with a per-machine reentrant lock via a no-op `_lock` hook the thread-safe subclass overrides, proven by a 50-thread Barrier test where exactly one operator wins. For distributed scale, I push that atomicity into a database compare-and-set."

▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Design a Vending Machine Leasing System. An operator LEASES physical vending machines for a term — this is the LEASING domain (lease terms, billing plans, machine availability), NOT the coin/dispense internals of a machine.

Core Requirements:

Register Machines: add vending machines (id, model, location) to the fleet.
Request Lease: an operator reserves an AVAILABLE machine under a billing term; a machine can back at most one non-terminal lease at a time.
Lease Lifecycle: a lease moves through a state machine — RESERVED → ACTIVE → EXPIRED/RETURNED, plus RESERVED → CANCELLED. Illegal transitions are refused.
Activate / Return / Cancel: start an active term, hand a machine back, or drop a reservation before it activates.
Billing Plans: compute a bill via a pluggable strategy (flat monthly vs revenue-share).
Expiry: leases expire automatically at term end and the machine is reclaimed.

Concurrency Requirement:

Two operators attempting to lease the SAME available machine simultaneously must resolve to EXACTLY ONE winner; the loser gets a clear "already leased" error. Use a per-machine lock.

Design Considerations:

Use Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles.
Identify key classes (e.g. LeasingService, Lease, VendingMachine, BillingStrategy, Operator, BillingTerm).
Model the Lease as a state machine.
Discuss design patterns (State, Strategy, Information Expert, Template Method).
Focus on extensibility for new billing plans and a clean per-machine concurrency model.

# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the Vending Machine Leasing System.

An operator LEASES a physical vending machine for a term. The interesting part
is the LEASE LIFECYCLE — a small state machine
(RESERVED -> ACTIVE -> EXPIRED / RETURNED, or RESERVED -> CANCELLED) — plus the
rule that a machine can back at most ONE non-terminal lease at a time.

Notice "AVAILABLE" is NOT a lease state at all. It is the machine's resting
condition, which we model as "no lease is holding me right now"
(current_lease_id is None). That single decision is what keeps the design clean.

🎙️ "The smart move is realising AVAILABLE belongs to the machine, not the lease.
The lease is the state machine; the machine just remembers WHICH lease holds it.
One source of truth, so the double-lease race becomes a one-line check."
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from enum import Enum


# ── VOCABULARY ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class LeaseStatus(Enum):
    RESERVED = "RESERVED"    # an operator has claimed the machine; term not started
    ACTIVE = "ACTIVE"        # the lease is running; the machine is deployed
    EXPIRED = "EXPIRED"      # the term ended naturally (reclaimed by the sweep)
    RETURNED = "RETURNED"    # the operator handed the machine back
    CANCELLED = "CANCELLED"  # the reservation was dropped before it ever activated


# The only transitions the lease state machine permits. Anything not listed here
# is an illegal jump and is refused — illegal states become impossible.
_LEGAL_TRANSITIONS: dict[LeaseStatus, frozenset[LeaseStatus]] = {
    LeaseStatus.RESERVED: frozenset({LeaseStatus.ACTIVE, LeaseStatus.CANCELLED}),
    LeaseStatus.ACTIVE: frozenset({LeaseStatus.RETURNED, LeaseStatus.EXPIRED}),
    LeaseStatus.EXPIRED: frozenset(),
    LeaseStatus.RETURNED: frozenset(),
    LeaseStatus.CANCELLED: frozenset(),
}

TERMINAL_STATES = frozenset(
    {LeaseStatus.EXPIRED, LeaseStatus.RETURNED, LeaseStatus.CANCELLED}
)


class BillingPlan(Enum):
    FLAT_MONTHLY = "FLAT_MONTHLY"    # fixed rate per month; revenue ignored
    REVENUE_SHARE = "REVENUE_SHARE"  # base fee plus a cut of reported revenue


# ── EXCEPTIONS (one base, one subclass per failure mode) ─────────────────────
class LeasingError(Exception):
    """Base for every error this system raises — callers catch one type."""


class DuplicateMachineError(LeasingError):
    """register_machine was called with an id already in the fleet."""


class UnknownMachineError(LeasingError):
    """A machine id that was never registered."""


class UnknownLeaseError(LeasingError):
    """A lease id the service never issued."""


class MachineNotAvailableError(LeasingError):
    """request_lease hit a machine that already has a non-terminal lease."""


class IllegalLeaseTransitionError(LeasingError):
    """An attempt to move a lease through a transition its state machine forbids."""


class InvalidLeaseTermError(LeasingError):
    """A billing term with a non-positive month count or out-of-range parameters."""


# ── VALUE OBJECTS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Operator:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The lessee — the company renting machines. Frozen: once we know who it is,
    # nothing about the identity changes, so Python stops accidental edits.
    operator_id: str
    name: str


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class BillingTerm:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The commercial terms of a lease: how long, which plan, and the numbers the
    # plan needs. Frozen — the deal is fixed the moment the lease is signed.
    # It carries the params for BOTH plans; the chosen `plan` decides which the
    # strategy reads. Money is in integer cents so there's no float drift.
    term_months: int
    plan: BillingPlan
    monthly_rate_cents: int = 0    # FLAT_MONTHLY: charged every month
    base_fee_cents: int = 0        # REVENUE_SHARE: fixed floor
    revenue_share_bps: int = 0     # REVENUE_SHARE: the cut in basis points (2000 = 20%)

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        # WHY: validate at construction so a bad term can never reach a Lease.
        if self.term_months < 1:
            raise InvalidLeaseTermError("term_months must be >= 1")
        if self.monthly_rate_cents < 0 or self.base_fee_cents < 0:
            raise InvalidLeaseTermError("fees must be non-negative")
        if not 0 <= self.revenue_share_bps <= 10_000:
            raise InvalidLeaseTermError("revenue_share_bps must be within 0..10000")


# ── ENTITIES ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class VendingMachine:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # A physical machine in the fleet. It remembers its OWN physical facts
    # (model, location) and exactly ONE relational fact: which lease holds it
    # right now. "Available" is not a stored flag — it's simply "no lease holds
    # me", so there is no second status that could drift out of sync.
    machine_id: str
    model: str
    location: str
    current_lease_id: str | None = None   # None == AVAILABLE

    def is_available(self) -> bool:
        return self.current_lease_id is None

    def hold(self, lease_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: this is the one check-then-act the whole concurrency story rides on.
        #      If somebody already holds me, refuse — the service turns this into
        #      a typed "already leased" error for the operator who lost the race.
        if self.current_lease_id is not None:
            raise MachineNotAvailableError(
                f"machine {self.machine_id} is already leased by {self.current_lease_id}"
            )
        self.current_lease_id = lease_id

    def release(self, lease_id: str) -> None:
        # WHY: safe to call twice — if I'm already free, or held by a DIFFERENT
        #      lease, the call does nothing. So a return racing the expiry sweep
        #      can never free a box out from under the wrong lease.
        if self.current_lease_id == lease_id:
            self.current_lease_id = None


@dataclass
class Lease:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The lease lifecycle state machine — the heart of the design. ONLY this
    # class changes a lease's status, and it refuses every illegal jump. The
    # service never writes `lease.status = ...` directly; it asks the lease to
    # transition itself, so the rules live in exactly one place.
    lease_id: str
    machine_id: str
    operator: Operator
    term: BillingTerm
    status: LeaseStatus = LeaseStatus.RESERVED
    started_at: datetime | None = None
    expires_at: datetime | None = None
    final_bill_cents: int | None = None

    def _transition(self, target: LeaseStatus) -> None:
        # WHY: one gate for every status change. If `target` is not reachable
        #      from where we are now, raise — illegal jumps become impossible.
        if target not in _LEGAL_TRANSITIONS[self.status]:
            raise IllegalLeaseTransitionError(
                f"lease {self.lease_id}: cannot go {self.status.value} -> {target.value}"
            )
        self.status = target

    def activate(self, now: datetime) -> None:
        # RESERVED -> ACTIVE; stamp the clock so the sweep knows the term end.
        self._transition(LeaseStatus.ACTIVE)
        self.started_at = now
        self.expires_at = now + timedelta(days=30 * self.term.term_months)

    def mark_returned(self) -> None:
        self._transition(LeaseStatus.RETURNED)

    def expire(self) -> None:
        self._transition(LeaseStatus.EXPIRED)

    def cancel(self) -> None:
        self._transition(LeaseStatus.CANCELLED)

    def is_due(self, now: datetime) -> bool:
        # WHY: only an ACTIVE lease past its term end is due. The sweep uses this
        #      to decide what to reclaim, so the test of "is it time?" lives with
        #      the lease that owns `expires_at`.
        return (
            self.status == LeaseStatus.ACTIVE
            and self.expires_at is not None
            and now >= self.expires_at
        )

# Python 3.11+
"""Billing strategies — the seam that decides HOW a lease is priced.

🎙️ "The service doesn't hardcode 'rent is monthly'. It asks a BillingStrategy
for the number. Flat-monthly today, revenue-share tomorrow, tiered next quarter —
each is a new class, zero service changes. The lease only stores WHICH plan; the
service maps the plan to the strategy object."

These are STATELESS: every number lives on the BillingTerm, so one shared
instance prices every lease on that plan.
"""

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

from models import BillingPlan, BillingTerm


class BillingStrategy(ABC):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The abstraction LeasingService depends on (Dependency Inversion). The
    # service never names a concrete plan — it just calls cost_cents on whatever
    # strategy the lease's plan maps to.
    plan: BillingPlan

    @abstractmethod
    def cost_cents(self, term: BillingTerm, reported_revenue_cents: int) -> int:
        """Total amount owed for this lease, in integer cents."""
        raise NotImplementedError


class FlatMonthly(BillingStrategy):
    """Fixed rent: the monthly rate times the number of months. Revenue ignored."""

    plan = BillingPlan.FLAT_MONTHLY

    def cost_cents(self, term: BillingTerm, reported_revenue_cents: int) -> int:
        return term.monthly_rate_cents * term.term_months


class RevenueShare(BillingStrategy):
    """A base floor plus a cut of the operator's reported revenue (basis points)."""

    plan = BillingPlan.REVENUE_SHARE

    def cost_cents(self, term: BillingTerm, reported_revenue_cents: int) -> int:
        # WHY: integer cents + basis points keeps money exact — no float drift.
        #      bps means "parts per 10,000", so 2000 bps == 20%.
        share = reported_revenue_cents * term.revenue_share_bps // 10_000
        return term.base_fee_cents + share

# Python 3.11+
"""LeasingService — the orchestrator — plus its thread-safe subclass.

The clean service is single-threaded-correct and carries ZERO lock code: the
`_lock` hook is a no-op context manager. The thread-safe subclass overrides just
that one method with a per-machine reentrant lock, so the whole diff between
"clean" and "concurrent" is about ten lines.
"""

import itertools
import threading
from collections.abc import Callable, Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager
from datetime import datetime

from models import (
    BillingPlan,
    BillingTerm,
    DuplicateMachineError,
    Lease,
    LeaseStatus,
    Operator,
    UnknownLeaseError,
    UnknownMachineError,
    VendingMachine,
)
from strategies import BillingStrategy, FlatMonthly, RevenueShare


class LeasingService:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The brain. It owns the fleet of machines, the lease registry, and the
    # plan -> strategy map. Every public operation flows through here. It never
    # writes a lease status by hand — it asks the Lease to transition itself.
    def __init__(
        self,
        billing: dict[BillingPlan, BillingStrategy] | None = None,
        now_fn: Callable[[], datetime] = datetime.now,
    ) -> None:
        self._machines: dict[str, VendingMachine] = {}
        self._leases: dict[str, Lease] = {}
        # WHY: the clock is injected so expiry tests can jump forward in time
        #      without actually sleeping for a month.
        self._now = now_fn
        # WHY: a counter gives readable ids (lease-1, lease-2) for tests; in
        #      production I'd swap in a UUID. next() on count() is atomic in
        #      CPython, so ids stay unique even under concurrent submits.
        self._lease_seq = itertools.count(1)
        # WHY: plan -> strategy is the Strategy/Factory seam. A new pricing rule
        #      is a new entry here plus a new class — the methods below never change.
        self._billing: dict[BillingPlan, BillingStrategy] = billing or {
            BillingPlan.FLAT_MONTHLY: FlatMonthly(),
            BillingPlan.REVENUE_SHARE: RevenueShare(),
        }

    # ── the no-op lock hook (a placeholder the subclass replaces) ──
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, machine: VendingMachine) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: does NOTHING in the clean version — there is not a single lock in
        #      this file. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this method.
        yield

    # ── registration ──
    def register_machine(self, machine_id: str, model: str, location: str) -> VendingMachine:
        if machine_id in self._machines:
            raise DuplicateMachineError(f"machine {machine_id} already registered")
        machine = VendingMachine(machine_id=machine_id, model=model, location=location)
        self._machines[machine_id] = machine
        return machine

    # ── the pipeline: claim an available machine ──
    def request_lease(self, machine_id: str, operator: Operator, term: BillingTerm) -> Lease:
        machine = self._require_machine(machine_id)
        lease = Lease(
            lease_id=f"lease-{next(self._lease_seq)}",
            machine_id=machine_id,
            operator=operator,
            term=term,
        )
        with self._lock(machine):
            # WHY: check-and-hold must be ONE atomic step. machine.hold raises if
            #      somebody already holds it — that is the "already leased" error.
            #      We only record the lease AFTER the hold succeeds, so a loser
            #      never leaves an orphan in the registry.
            machine.hold(lease.lease_id)
            self._leases[lease.lease_id] = lease
        return lease

    def activate_lease(self, lease_id: str) -> Lease:
        lease = self._require_lease(lease_id)
        machine = self._require_machine(lease.machine_id)
        with self._lock(machine):
            lease.activate(self._now())   # RESERVED -> ACTIVE (guarded by the lease)
        return lease

    def return_lease(self, lease_id: str, reported_revenue_cents: int = 0) -> Lease:
        lease = self._require_lease(lease_id)
        machine = self._require_machine(lease.machine_id)
        with self._lock(machine):
            lease.mark_returned()                  # ACTIVE -> RETURNED (guarded)
            machine.release(lease.lease_id)         # machine is AVAILABLE again
            # WHY: cutting the closing invoice re-reads the lease's status under
            #      the lock, so this RE-ENTERS _lock(machine). That is exactly why
            #      the thread-safe version uses an RLock, not a plain Lock.
            lease.final_bill_cents = self.compute_bill(lease_id, reported_revenue_cents)
        return lease

    def cancel_lease(self, lease_id: str) -> Lease:
        lease = self._require_lease(lease_id)
        machine = self._require_machine(lease.machine_id)
        with self._lock(machine):
            lease.cancel()                          # RESERVED -> CANCELLED (guarded)
            machine.release(lease.lease_id)
        return lease

    def list_available(self) -> list[VendingMachine]:
        # WHY: availability is derived, not stored — a machine is free exactly
        #      when no lease holds it. One source of truth, nothing to keep in sync.
        return [m for m in self._machines.values() if m.is_available()]

    def compute_bill(self, lease_id: str, reported_revenue_cents: int = 0) -> int:
        lease = self._require_lease(lease_id)
        machine = self._require_machine(lease.machine_id)
        with self._lock(machine):
            # WHY: reads the lease's mutable status under the lock. A reservation
            #      that was cancelled before it ran never owes anything.
            if lease.status == LeaseStatus.CANCELLED:
                return 0
            strategy = self._billing[lease.term.plan]
            return strategy.cost_cents(lease.term, reported_revenue_cents)

    def sweep_expired(self) -> list[Lease]:
        # WHY: a background timer calls this to reclaim leases whose term ended.
        #      Idempotent on purpose — a second pass finds nothing due and is a
        #      no-op, so it is safe to run on a schedule.
        now = self._now()
        expired: list[Lease] = []
        for machine in list(self._machines.values()):
            with self._lock(machine):
                lease_id = machine.current_lease_id
                if lease_id is None:
                    continue
                lease = self._leases.get(lease_id)
                if lease is not None and lease.is_due(now):
                    lease.expire()                  # ACTIVE -> EXPIRED (guarded)
                    machine.release(lease.lease_id)
                    expired.append(lease)
        return expired

    # ── private helpers ──
    def _require_machine(self, machine_id: str) -> VendingMachine:
        machine = self._machines.get(machine_id)
        if machine is None:
            raise UnknownMachineError(f"no machine with id {machine_id}")
        return machine

    def _require_lease(self, lease_id: str) -> Lease:
        lease = self._leases.get(lease_id)
        if lease is None:
            raise UnknownLeaseError(f"no lease with id {lease_id}")
        return lease


class ThreadSafeLeasingService(LeasingService):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The ONLY thing concurrency changes. It overrides `_lock` with a real
    # per-machine reentrant lock. Everything in LeasingService above is untouched.
    def __init__(
        self,
        billing: dict[BillingPlan, BillingStrategy] | None = None,
        now_fn: Callable[[], datetime] = datetime.now,
    ) -> None:
        super().__init__(billing=billing, now_fn=now_fn)
        self._locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}
        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()   # guards the dict of locks itself

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, machine: VendingMachine) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: lock the MAP first so two threads creating the same machine's lock
        #      can't corrupt the dict, grab the per-machine RLock, then drop the
        #      map lock and hold only the machine lock. RLock (not Lock) because
        #      return_lease holds it and calls compute_bill, which re-takes it.
        with self._map_lock:
            lock = self._locks.setdefault(machine.machine_id, threading.RLock())
        with lock:
            yield

# Python 3.11+
"""Narrated demo driver for the Vending Machine Leasing System.

Run: `python3 main.py`. Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK" so a
green run is obvious at a glance.
"""

import threading

from leasing_service import LeasingService, ThreadSafeLeasingService
from models import (
    BillingPlan,
    BillingTerm,
    IllegalLeaseTransitionError,
    LeaseStatus,
    MachineNotAvailableError,
    Operator,
)


def main() -> None:
    acme = Operator(operator_id="op-acme", name="Acme Snacks")
    flat = BillingTerm(term_months=6, plan=BillingPlan.FLAT_MONTHLY, monthly_rate_cents=20_000)

    # ── Case 1: full happy lifecycle — request -> activate -> bill -> return ──
    svc = LeasingService()
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", model="SnackMax 3000", location="Lobby A")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    assert lease.status == LeaseStatus.RESERVED, lease.status
    svc.activate_lease(lease.lease_id)
    assert lease.status == LeaseStatus.ACTIVE, lease.status
    bill = svc.compute_bill(lease.lease_id)
    print(f"Case 1: lease {lease.lease_id} ACTIVE; 6-month flat bill = {bill} cents (${bill / 100:.2f})")
    assert bill == 120_000, bill
    svc.return_lease(lease.lease_id)
    assert lease.status == LeaseStatus.RETURNED
    assert [m.machine_id for m in svc.list_available()] == ["VM-1"]
    print("Case 1: request -> activate -> bill -> return; machine free again -> OK")

    # ── Case 2: revenue-share billing math (base + cut of revenue) ──
    svc.register_machine("VM-2", model="FizzPour 1", location="Gym")
    rev = BillingTerm(
        term_months=12, plan=BillingPlan.REVENUE_SHARE,
        base_fee_cents=5_000, revenue_share_bps=2_000,   # 20%
    )
    l2 = svc.request_lease("VM-2", acme, rev)
    svc.activate_lease(l2.lease_id)
    bill2 = svc.compute_bill(l2.lease_id, reported_revenue_cents=1_000_000)  # $10,000 revenue
    print(f"Case 2: revenue-share bill = base 5000 + 20% of 1,000,000 = {bill2} cents")
    assert bill2 == 5_000 + 200_000, bill2
    print("Case 2: revenue-share strategy computes base + 20% cut -> OK")

    # ── Case 3: cancel a reservation before it ever activates ──
    svc.register_machine("VM-3", model="SnackMax 3000", location="Lobby B")
    l3 = svc.request_lease("VM-3", acme, flat)
    svc.cancel_lease(l3.lease_id)
    assert l3.status == LeaseStatus.CANCELLED
    assert any(m.machine_id == "VM-3" for m in svc.list_available())
    print("Case 3: a RESERVED lease cancels cleanly and frees its machine -> OK")

    # ── Case 4: the state machine refuses an illegal transition ──
    l4 = svc.request_lease("VM-3", acme, flat)
    svc.activate_lease(l4.lease_id)
    try:
        svc.cancel_lease(l4.lease_id)            # cannot cancel an ACTIVE lease
        assert False, "should have refused ACTIVE -> CANCELLED"
    except IllegalLeaseTransitionError as e:
        print(f"Case 4: cancel on an ACTIVE lease refused -> {e}")
    svc.return_lease(l4.lease_id)                # the legal exit is a return
    print("Case 4: the lease state machine blocks ACTIVE -> CANCELLED -> OK")

    # ── Case 5: two operators race for the SAME machine — exactly one wins ──
    tsvc = ThreadSafeLeasingService()
    tsvc.register_machine("VM-HOT", model="SnackMax 3000", location="Food Court")
    wins: list = []
    losses: list = []
    barrier = threading.Barrier(2)
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def attempt(i: int) -> None:
        op = Operator(operator_id=f"op-{i}", name=f"Operator {i}")
        barrier.wait()                            # both threads fire at one instant
        try:
            lease = tsvc.request_lease("VM-HOT", op, flat)
            with guard:
                wins.append(lease)
        except MachineNotAvailableError as e:
            with guard:
                losses.append(str(e))

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(i,)) for i in range(2)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    print(f"Case 5: 2 operators raced VM-HOT -> {len(wins)} won, {len(losses)} rejected ({losses[0]})")
    assert len(wins) == 1, len(wins)
    assert len(losses) == 1
    assert all(m.machine_id != "VM-HOT" for m in tsvc.list_available())
    print("Case 5: contended double-lease — exactly one operator wins -> OK")

    print("\nAll cases passed.")

import pathlib
import sys

# Put the workspace root on sys.path so `from models import ...` works in tests.
sys.path.insert(0, str(pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent))

# Python 3.11+
"""pytest suite for the Vending Machine Leasing System.

Covers: the lease lifecycle state machine (every legal transition + every illegal
one), billing correctness for both strategies, every typed error path, expiry via
the background sweep, idempotency, and THREE concurrency proofs that use a
threading.Barrier so all threads hit request_lease at one instant:
  * two operators race the same machine            -> exactly one wins
  * fifty operators race the same machine          -> exactly one wins
  * fifty operators race five machines             -> exactly five win, all distinct
"""

import threading
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

import pytest

from leasing_service import LeasingService, ThreadSafeLeasingService
from models import (
    BillingPlan,
    BillingTerm,
    DuplicateMachineError,
    IllegalLeaseTransitionError,
    InvalidLeaseTermError,
    LeaseStatus,
    MachineNotAvailableError,
    Operator,
    UnknownLeaseError,
    UnknownMachineError,
)


# ── fixtures ──
@pytest.fixture
def clock():
    """A mutable clock so expiry tests can jump forward without sleeping."""
    return {"t": datetime(2026, 1, 1, 9, 0, 0)}


@pytest.fixture
def svc(clock):
    return LeasingService(now_fn=lambda: clock["t"])


@pytest.fixture
def acme():
    return Operator(operator_id="op-acme", name="Acme Snacks")


@pytest.fixture
def flat():
    return BillingTerm(term_months=6, plan=BillingPlan.FLAT_MONTHLY, monthly_rate_cents=20_000)


@pytest.fixture
def revshare():
    return BillingTerm(
        term_months=12, plan=BillingPlan.REVENUE_SHARE,
        base_fee_cents=5_000, revenue_share_bps=2_000,
    )


# ── registration ──
def test_register_machine_returns_available_machine(svc):
    m = svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    assert m.is_available()
    assert svc.list_available() == [m]


def test_duplicate_machine_rejected(svc):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    with pytest.raises(DuplicateMachineError):
        svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")


# ── happy-path lifecycle ──
def test_request_lease_reserves_and_holds_machine(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    assert lease.status == LeaseStatus.RESERVED
    assert svc._machines["VM-1"].current_lease_id == lease.lease_id
    assert svc.list_available() == []                    # the machine is taken


def test_activate_then_return_full_cycle(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    svc.activate_lease(lease.lease_id)
    assert lease.status == LeaseStatus.ACTIVE
    returned = svc.return_lease(lease.lease_id)
    assert returned.status == LeaseStatus.RETURNED
    assert returned.final_bill_cents == 120_000          # closing invoice stamped
    assert svc.list_available()[0].machine_id == "VM-1"  # machine free again


def test_cancel_reserved_lease_frees_machine(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    svc.cancel_lease(lease.lease_id)
    assert lease.status == LeaseStatus.CANCELLED
    assert svc.list_available()[0].machine_id == "VM-1"


def test_reuse_machine_after_return(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    first = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    svc.activate_lease(first.lease_id)
    svc.return_lease(first.lease_id)
    second = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)        # same machine, new lease
    assert second.lease_id != first.lease_id
    assert second.status == LeaseStatus.RESERVED


# ── illegal transitions (state machine guards) ──
def test_cannot_cancel_active_lease(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    svc.activate_lease(lease.lease_id)
    with pytest.raises(IllegalLeaseTransitionError):
        svc.cancel_lease(lease.lease_id)                 # ACTIVE -> CANCELLED forbidden


def test_cannot_return_reserved_lease(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    with pytest.raises(IllegalLeaseTransitionError):
        svc.return_lease(lease.lease_id)                 # must activate first


def test_cannot_activate_twice(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    svc.activate_lease(lease.lease_id)
    with pytest.raises(IllegalLeaseTransitionError):
        svc.activate_lease(lease.lease_id)               # ACTIVE -> ACTIVE forbidden


def test_cannot_act_on_terminal_lease(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    svc.cancel_lease(lease.lease_id)
    with pytest.raises(IllegalLeaseTransitionError):
        svc.activate_lease(lease.lease_id)               # CANCELLED is terminal


# ── machine availability errors ──
def test_request_on_held_machine_rejected(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    with pytest.raises(MachineNotAvailableError):
        svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)            # already leased


def test_unknown_machine_rejected(svc, acme, flat):
    with pytest.raises(UnknownMachineError):
        svc.request_lease("NOPE", acme, flat)


def test_unknown_lease_rejected(svc):
    with pytest.raises(UnknownLeaseError):
        svc.activate_lease("lease-999")


# ── billing correctness ──
def test_flat_monthly_billing(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    assert svc.compute_bill(lease.lease_id) == 20_000 * 6


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "revenue,expected",
    [(0, 5_000), (1_000_000, 5_000 + 200_000), (250_000, 5_000 + 50_000)],
)
def test_revenue_share_billing(svc, acme, revshare, revenue, expected):
    svc.register_machine("VM-2", "FizzPour", "Gym")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-2", acme, revshare)
    assert svc.compute_bill(lease.lease_id, reported_revenue_cents=revenue) == expected


def test_cancelled_lease_owes_nothing(svc, acme, flat):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    svc.cancel_lease(lease.lease_id)
    assert svc.compute_bill(lease.lease_id) == 0


def test_invalid_term_rejected():
    with pytest.raises(InvalidLeaseTermError):
        BillingTerm(term_months=0, plan=BillingPlan.FLAT_MONTHLY, monthly_rate_cents=1)
    with pytest.raises(InvalidLeaseTermError):
        BillingTerm(term_months=1, plan=BillingPlan.REVENUE_SHARE, revenue_share_bps=10_001)


# ── expiry via the background sweep (+ idempotency) ──
def test_sweep_expires_due_active_leases(svc, acme, flat, clock):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    svc.activate_lease(lease.lease_id)
    clock["t"] += timedelta(days=30 * 6 + 1)             # jump past the 6-month term
    expired = svc.sweep_expired()
    assert [l.lease_id for l in expired] == [lease.lease_id]
    assert lease.status == LeaseStatus.EXPIRED
    assert svc.list_available()[0].machine_id == "VM-1"  # reclaimed


def test_sweep_is_idempotent(svc, acme, flat, clock):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    svc.activate_lease(lease.lease_id)
    clock["t"] += timedelta(days=30 * 6 + 1)
    assert len(svc.sweep_expired()) == 1
    assert svc.sweep_expired() == []                     # second pass: nothing due


def test_active_lease_not_yet_due_is_left_alone(svc, acme, flat, clock):
    svc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    lease = svc.request_lease("VM-1", acme, flat)
    svc.activate_lease(lease.lease_id)
    clock["t"] += timedelta(days=10)                     # still inside the term
    assert svc.sweep_expired() == []
    assert lease.status == LeaseStatus.ACTIVE


# ── concurrency proofs (Barrier => all threads race at one instant) ──
def _race_for_machine(tsvc, machine_id, n, term):
    """Fire `n` operators at one machine simultaneously; return (wins, losses)."""
    wins: list = []
    losses: list = []
    guard = threading.Lock()
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def attempt(i):
        op = Operator(operator_id=f"op-{machine_id}-{i}", name="op")
        barrier.wait()
        try:
            lease = tsvc.request_lease(machine_id, op, term)
            with guard:
                wins.append(lease)
        except MachineNotAvailableError as e:
            with guard:
                losses.append(str(e))

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(i,)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()
    return wins, losses


def test_two_operators_same_machine_exactly_one_wins(flat):
    tsvc = ThreadSafeLeasingService()
    tsvc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    wins, losses = _race_for_machine(tsvc, "VM-1", 2, flat)
    assert len(wins) == 1
    assert len(losses) == 1
    assert "already leased" in losses[0]
    assert wins[0].status == LeaseStatus.RESERVED
    assert tsvc._machines["VM-1"].current_lease_id == wins[0].lease_id


def test_fifty_operators_same_machine_exactly_one_wins(flat):
    tsvc = ThreadSafeLeasingService()
    tsvc.register_machine("VM-1", "SnackMax", "Lobby")
    wins, losses = _race_for_machine(tsvc, "VM-1", 50, flat)
    assert len(wins) == 1                                  # exactly one winner
    assert len(losses) == 49                               # everyone else rejected
    assert all(isinstance(msg, str) and "already leased" in msg for msg in losses)
    held = [m for m in tsvc._machines.values() if not m.is_available()]
    assert len(held) == 1                                  # one machine now held


def test_fifty_operators_five_machines_unique_winners(flat):
    tsvc = ThreadSafeLeasingService()
    machine_ids = [f"VM-{i}" for i in range(5)]
    for mid in machine_ids:
        tsvc.register_machine(mid, "SnackMax", "Court")

    wins: list = []
    losses: list = []
    guard = threading.Lock()
    per_machine = 10
    n = len(machine_ids) * per_machine                     # 50 threads total
    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)

    def attempt(mid, k):
        op = Operator(operator_id=f"op-{mid}-{k}", name="op")
        barrier.wait()
        try:
            lease = tsvc.request_lease(mid, op, flat)
            with guard:
                wins.append(lease)
        except MachineNotAvailableError as e:
            with guard:
                losses.append(str(e))

    threads = [
        threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(mid, k))
        for mid in machine_ids
        for k in range(per_machine)
    ]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    assert len(wins) == 5                                   # exactly one per machine
    assert len({w.lease_id for w in wins}) == 5             # unique lease ids
    assert len({w.machine_id for w in wins}) == 5           # each on a DISTINCT machine
    assert len(losses) == n - 5
    held = [m for m in tsvc._machines.values() if not m.is_available()]
    assert len(held) == 5                                   # all five machines held

All tests pass — here's the complete, interview-ready design.

> 🎙️ **Open with this framing** (the senior move for this problem): "This is NOT a vending machine that dispenses snacks — it's a *leasing* system. An operator rents a physical machine for a term. So the domain is leases, terms, and billing, and the heart of it is a tiny state machine: a lease goes RESERVED → ACTIVE → RETURNED or EXPIRED, or RESERVED → CANCELLED. The one trap is that two operators can race for the same free machine, so I'll guard each machine with its own lock."

## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan

| Time | What you do | Mode |
|------|-------------|------|
| 0–5 min | **Say §1–2 out loud** — restate that this is a *leasing* domain (not snack-dispensing), ask clarifying questions, lock scope. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — state the 7 operator-facing methods + the background sweep before designing classes. "Here's the contract external code calls." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — only `LeasingService`, `Lease` (state machine), `VendingMachine`, `BillingStrategy`. Mention `Operator`/`BillingTerm` verbally as data holders. | ✍️ DRAW |
| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — State (lease), Strategy (billing), then immediately: "There's a check-then-act race in `request_lease` — two operators see the same machine free. I'll fix it with a per-machine lock in §9." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — the key insight (AVAILABLE belongs to the machine, not the lease) + the `request_lease` pipeline in plain words. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** (§6): enums → `Lease` state machine → `BillingStrategy` → `LeasingService` skeleton → `request_lease()` → `return_lease()`. | ✍️ WRITE |
| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 5** — happy lifecycle + contended double-lease. Run `main.py` if it's a coding env. | 🗣️ SAY / run |
| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — per-machine `RLock` via the no-op `_lock` hook. Write the subclass only if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
| 55–60 min | **Follow-up questions** — use the hardest follow-ups from §9 (distributed CAS, 10M machines, partial-failure). | ❓ IF ASKED |

**Two interview modes — pick one up front:**
- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** skip §7 run output, demo cases verbally, write code but don't execute.
- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** write and RUN `main.py` in §7; show actual terminal output (what I did below).

**The one thing that flags you "strong hire":** proactively say "I see a check-then-act race in `request_lease`" right after the diagram — before being asked. It signals you've built real concurrent systems.

## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions

### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)

Think of a company that owns a fleet of physical vending machines and *rents them out* to operators — a snack company leases ten machines for an office tower, runs them for a 12-month term, and pays either flat monthly rent or a share of the revenue. Our system is the back office for that: it tracks which machines are free, lets an operator reserve and activate a lease, computes the bill, and reclaims a machine when the lease ends. The interesting part isn't dispensing snacks — it's the **lease lifecycle** and making sure two operators can't lease the same machine at once.

### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)

**You:** "Just to be clear on scope — this is a *leasing* system, not the machine's coin/dispense internals, right? I'd model leases, terms, and billing, and treat the snack-dispensing electronics as a separate downstream system. Sound right?"
**Interviewer:** "Exactly — leasing only."
*✓ scoped: domain is leases/terms/billing; dispense internals are out.*

**You:** "I'll assume the core operator actions are: reserve a free machine, activate the lease, return it, and cancel a reservation that hasn't started. Plus the back office registers machines and pulls a bill. Is that the v1 surface?"
**Interviewer:** "Yep, that's the set."
*✓ locked the 7-method surface; everything else is internal.*

**You:** "For the lease lifecycle I'd use one state machine: RESERVED → ACTIVE → then either RETURNED or EXPIRED, and RESERVED → CANCELLED. You can't cancel an active lease — you return it. Agreed?"
**Interviewer:** "Agreed — and make expiry automatic at term end."
*✓ added: a background sweep reclaims machines whose term elapsed → §9 / `sweep_expired`.*

**You:** "Billing: I'll support two plans for v1 — flat monthly rent, and a revenue-share (base fee + a percentage cut). I'll model that as a Strategy so a third plan is a new class, not an `if/else`. OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Good, two plans is fine."
*✓ Strategy seam earned; money in integer cents to avoid float drift.*

**You:** "Concurrency: the one real hazard is two operators reserving the *same* free machine simultaneously. I'd give each machine its own lock so different machines lease in parallel, and serialize only same-machine requests. I'll keep §6 single-threaded-clean and show the lock in §9. Reasonable?"
**Interviewer:** "That's exactly what I want to see."
*✓ per-machine lock, not one global lock; proven with a Barrier test.*

**You:** "Payments, installation logistics, notifications, and multi-region — all out of scope for this round, right? We just return the bill amount; a downstream system charges it."
**Interviewer:** "Correct, out of scope."
*✓ clean system boundary; we compute, we don't collect.*

### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)

**Functional Requirements**

1. **Register a machine** into the fleet by id, model, and location.
   - Returns the machine; rejects a duplicate id with a typed error.
2. **Request a lease** on an available machine for an operator under a billing term.
   - Reserves the machine (RESERVED lease) and holds it; if the machine already has a non-terminal lease, reject with "already leased".
3. **Activate a reserved lease** — RESERVED → ACTIVE; stamps the start and term-end.
   - Illegal from any non-RESERVED state.
4. **Return an active lease** — ACTIVE → RETURNED; frees the machine and stamps a closing bill.
   - Illegal from any non-ACTIVE state; accepts reported revenue for a revenue-share settlement.
5. **Cancel a reserved lease** — RESERVED → CANCELLED; frees the machine.
   - Illegal once the lease is ACTIVE (must return instead).
6. **List available machines** — every machine no lease currently holds.
7. **Compute a bill** for a lease via its plan's strategy (flat or revenue-share).
   - A cancelled-before-activation lease owes nothing.
8. **Expire leases at term end** — a background sweep moves due ACTIVE leases to EXPIRED and reclaims the machine. (Idempotent.)

**Out of scope**

- Coin handling / snack dispensing electronics (separate downstream hardware system).
- Payment collection (we return the amount owed; a billing system charges it).
- Installation/delivery logistics (physical ops, outside the boundary).
- Notifications, multi-region sharding, operator authentication (downstream / separate).

> 💬 **What you say to open §2:** "Before I model anything, the one thing I want locked is that AVAILABLE is a property of the *machine*, not the lease — the lease never has an AVAILABLE state. With that, let me walk the actors and flows."

## 2. Actors & Core Flows

**Actors**

- **Operator (lessee)** — the company renting machines; initiates reserve, activate, return, cancel.
- **Fleet admin (back office)** — registers machines and pulls bills.
- **Expiry sweep (background job)** — a timer-driven actor that reclaims machines whose lease term has elapsed.

**Core flows**

1. Admin: register machine → machine joins fleet as AVAILABLE
2. Operator: request lease → system reserves a free machine → RESERVED lease returned
3. Operator: activate → lease ACTIVE, term clock starts → … → return → machine free, closing bill stamped
4. Operator: cancel a RESERVED lease → machine free again, lease CANCELLED
5. Sweep: term elapsed → lease EXPIRED → machine reclaimed automatically

## 3. Core Entities (accept / reject)

### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough

> **VendingMachine** — A real physical thing in the fleet with an id, a model, and a location, plus the relational fact of which lease holds it right now. It has both data and behavior (hold/release). *Accepted.*
>
> **Lease** — The contract between an operator and a machine for a term. It owns a lifecycle (RESERVED → ACTIVE → …) and the rules for which transitions are legal. This is the state machine — the most behavior-rich object in the design. *Accepted.*
>
> **Operator** — The lessee. It's referenced by every lease, but it has no behavior of its own — just identity (id + name). It's a value object the caller hands in. *Accepted as a value object* (frozen, no behavior).
>
> **BillingTerm** — The commercial terms of a lease: months, plan, and the numbers the plan needs. It never changes once the lease is signed, and it's referenced by exactly one lease. *Accepted as a value object* (frozen).
>
> **BillingStrategy** — Not a noun from the prompt, but the *behavior* of "how do we price this?" varies (flat vs revenue-share). Pulling it into its own interface is what keeps the service from hardcoding pricing. *Accepted as a strategy interface* (lives in `strategies.py`).
>
> **AVAILABLE / "availability"** — Tempting to model as a machine status enum (AVAILABLE/LEASED). But that creates a *second* source of truth that can drift from the lease's status. A machine is available exactly when no lease holds it. *Rejected as a stored field — derived from `current_lease_id is None`.*
>
> **Bill / Invoice** — Tempting to make a class. But a bill is just a computed integer (cents) for a lease at a moment in time; it has no independent lifecycle. *Rejected — it's the return value of `compute_bill`, stamped onto the lease as `final_bill_cents`.*
>
> **Snack / Inventory / Coin** — The machine's *contents*. Our system leases the box, not its stock. *Rejected — downstream hardware concern, out of scope.*
>
> **Term length / Plan / Money** — Finite vocabularies or primitives. *Rejected as entities — `term_months: int`, a `BillingPlan` enum, and integer cents.*

### 3.2 Final entity table (accepted entities only)

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
|---|---|---|---|
| `VendingMachine` | Track one physical machine and which lease holds it | `machine_id: str` (id), `model: str`, `location: str`, `current_lease_id: str \| None` (ref → the holding lease) | At most one lease holds it; `hold()` refuses a second |
| `Lease` | The lifecycle state machine of one rental | `lease_id: str` (id), `machine_id: str` (ref → machine), `operator: Operator`, `term: BillingTerm`, `status: LeaseStatus`, `expires_at: datetime \| None` | Only legal transitions occur; illegal jumps raise |
| `Operator` (VO) | Identify the lessee | `operator_id: str` (id), `name: str` | Immutable identity (frozen) |
| `BillingTerm` (VO) | Hold the fixed commercial terms | `term_months: int`, `plan: BillingPlan`, `monthly_rate_cents: int`, `base_fee_cents: int`, `revenue_share_bps: int` | Months ≥ 1, fees ≥ 0, bps in 0..10000 (validated at construction) |
| `BillingStrategy` | Compute the amount owed for a plan | (stateless; reads `BillingTerm`) | Pricing logic lives behind one interface |

**Enums**
- `LeaseStatus`: `RESERVED`, `ACTIVE`, `EXPIRED`, `RETURNED`, `CANCELLED`
- `BillingPlan`: `FLAT_MONTHLY`, `REVENUE_SHARE`

### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro

> "OK so the model has four objects plus one strategy interface. `LeasingService` is the brain — it owns the fleet, the lease registry, and the plan-to-strategy map. `VendingMachine` is the physical thing; it remembers one relational fact — which lease holds it — and 'available' is just the absence of that. `Lease` is the interesting one because it's a state machine that guards its own transitions; the service never sets a status by hand. `BillingStrategy` is the pricing seam. The pieces I deliberately left out are a Bill class — that's just a computed integer — and a machine *status* enum, because availability is derived from the lease, so there's no second source of truth to keep in sync. Snacks and coins live in the downstream hardware system, not here."

## 3.5 API / System Interface (the public surface, before the classes)

The orchestrator's public surface — seven operator/admin-facing methods plus one background sweep. Every method traces to a §1.3 requirement.

| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `register_machine` | `(machine_id: str, model: str, location: str) -> VendingMachine` | the registered machine | `DuplicateMachineError` | Admin adds a machine to the fleet (FR1) |
| `request_lease` | `(machine_id: str, operator: Operator, term: BillingTerm) -> Lease` | a RESERVED lease | `UnknownMachineError`, `MachineNotAvailableError` | Operator claims a free machine (FR2) — the contended call |
| `activate_lease` | `(lease_id: str) -> Lease` | the now-ACTIVE lease | `UnknownLeaseError`, `IllegalLeaseTransitionError` | Operator starts the term (FR3) |
| `return_lease` | `(lease_id: str, reported_revenue_cents: int = 0) -> Lease` | the RETURNED lease (closing bill stamped) | `UnknownLeaseError`, `IllegalLeaseTransitionError` | Operator hands the machine back (FR4) |
| `cancel_lease` | `(lease_id: str) -> Lease` | the CANCELLED lease | `UnknownLeaseError`, `IllegalLeaseTransitionError` | Operator drops a reservation (FR5) |
| `list_available` | `() -> list[VendingMachine]` | machines no lease holds | — | Show what's leasable (FR6) |
| `compute_bill` | `(lease_id: str, reported_revenue_cents: int = 0) -> int` | amount owed, in cents | `UnknownLeaseError` | Quote/invoice a lease via its plan (FR7) |
| `sweep_expired` | `() -> list[Lease]` | leases just expired | — | Background reclaim of due leases (FR8) |

> 🎙️ "It's seven operator-facing calls plus one background sweep — right at the upper edge of what I'd call clean, so I'd watch it for growth. Notice everything contended funnels through `request_lease`; the lease transitions itself, the strategy prices itself, and 'available' is just `list_available` reading derived state. If you handed me this interface cold, you'd know exactly what the system does without seeing a class."

## 4. Class Design (derive each class, then the diagram)

### 4.1 Per-class derivation

#### `LeasingService` — the orchestrator

**(a) Role:** the system's public API and coordinator — external code calls it to register, lease, activate, return, cancel, list, and bill. Every operation flows through here.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "Register a machine into the fleet" (FR1) | a map: machine_id → VendingMachine |
| "Request/activate/return/cancel a lease" (FR2–5) | a map: lease_id → Lease |
| "Compute a bill via its plan" (FR7) | a map: BillingPlan → BillingStrategy |
| "Stamp the term clock" (FR3) / testability | an injected clock `now_fn` |
| readable lease ids | a monotonic counter |

```
class LeasingService:
    - _machines: Map<str, VendingMachine>
    - _leases: Map<str, Lease>
    - _billing: Map<BillingPlan, BillingStrategy>
    - _now: () -> datetime
    - _lease_seq: counter
```

*State placement trade-off:* a machine's *availability* could live on the orchestrator (a `set` of free ids) OR on the entity. I keep it on the entity as `current_lease_id` (relational state describing the machine's condition) and derive availability — that way there's exactly one source of truth and no free-set to keep in sync with the lease registry.

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| FR1 register a machine | `register_machine(machine_id, model, location) -> VendingMachine` |
| FR2 claim a free machine | `request_lease(machine_id, operator, term) -> Lease` |
| FR3 start the term | `activate_lease(lease_id) -> Lease` |
| FR4 hand it back | `return_lease(lease_id, reported_revenue_cents=0) -> Lease` |
| FR5 drop a reservation | `cancel_lease(lease_id) -> Lease` |
| FR6 list leasable machines | `list_available() -> list[VendingMachine]` |
| FR7 price a lease | `compute_bill(lease_id, reported_revenue_cents=0) -> int` |
| FR8 reclaim due leases | `sweep_expired() -> list[Lease]` |

```
class LeasingService:
    - _machines: Map<str, VendingMachine>
    - _leases: Map<str, Lease>
    - _billing: Map<BillingPlan, BillingStrategy>
    - _now: () -> datetime
    - _lease_seq: counter

    + LeasingService(billing=None, now_fn=datetime.now)
    + register_machine(machine_id, model, location) -> VendingMachine
    + request_lease(machine_id, operator, term) -> Lease
    + activate_lease(lease_id) -> Lease
    + return_lease(lease_id, reported_revenue_cents=0) -> Lease
    + cancel_lease(lease_id) -> Lease
    + list_available() -> VendingMachine[]
    + compute_bill(lease_id, reported_revenue_cents=0) -> int
    + sweep_expired() -> Lease[]
    # _lock(machine) -> contextmanager      (no-op hook; subclass overrides)
    # _require_machine(id) / _require_lease(id)
```

**(d) Design choices worth calling out**
- **Why does `request_lease` build the `Lease` *before* taking the lock?** The id and object construction touch no shared state, so doing them outside the lock keeps the critical section tiny — just `hold()` + register.
- **Why is `_lock(machine)` a no-op here?** So this file has zero lock code. The thread-safe subclass overrides only that one method — the clean→concurrent diff is ~10 lines.
- **Why does `return_lease` call `compute_bill` internally?** Returning cuts the closing invoice, and that re-reads the lease under the same machine lock — which is exactly why the real lock is *reentrant*.

#### `Lease` — the lifecycle state machine

**(a) Role:** the heart of the design — the only class allowed to change a lease's status, and it refuses every illegal jump.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "lifecycle RESERVED→ACTIVE→…" (FR2–5,8) | `status: LeaseStatus` |
| "which machine / operator / term" | `machine_id`, `operator`, `term` |
| "stamp start + term end" (FR3) | `started_at`, `expires_at` |
| "closing bill on return" (FR4) | `final_bill_cents` |

```
class Lease:
    - lease_id: str
    - machine_id: str
    - operator: Operator
    - term: BillingTerm
    - status: LeaseStatus = RESERVED
    - started_at: datetime | None
    - expires_at: datetime | None
    - final_bill_cents: int | None
```

*State placement trade-off:* the legal-transition table could live on the service, but transition rules are *intrinsic* to a lease — so the lease owns them (Information Expert). The service only decides *when* to ask for a transition, never *whether* it's legal.

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| start the term (FR3) | `activate(now) -> None` |
| hand it back (FR4) | `mark_returned() -> None` |
| auto-reclaim at term end (FR8) | `expire() -> None` |
| drop a reservation (FR5) | `cancel() -> None` |
| sweep decision (FR8) | `is_due(now) -> bool` |
| enforce the rules | `_transition(target) -> None` (private gate) |

```
class Lease:
    - lease_id: str ; machine_id: str ; operator: Operator ; term: BillingTerm
    - status: LeaseStatus = RESERVED
    - started_at: datetime | None ; expires_at: datetime | None
    - final_bill_cents: int | None

    + activate(now: datetime) -> None      # RESERVED -> ACTIVE, stamp clock
    + mark_returned() -> None              # ACTIVE -> RETURNED
    + expire() -> None                     # ACTIVE -> EXPIRED
    + cancel() -> None                     # RESERVED -> CANCELLED
    + is_due(now: datetime) -> bool        # ACTIVE and past expires_at
    - _transition(target: LeaseStatus)     # the one legal-transition gate
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why one `_transition` gate?** Every public transition routes through it, so the legal-transition table is enforced in exactly one place — adding a state can't bypass a check.
- **Why does `activate` take `now` as a parameter?** The clock is injected from the service so expiry is testable without sleeping a month.
- **Why is `is_due` on the lease, not the sweep?** The lease owns `expires_at`, so it's the expert on whether it's due.

#### `VendingMachine` — the physical resource

**(a) Role:** represents one machine and the single relational fact of which lease holds it.

**(b) State derivation**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "register by id/model/location" (FR1) | `machine_id`, `model`, `location` |
| "at most one lease holds it" (FR2) | `current_lease_id: str \| None` |

```
class VendingMachine:
    - machine_id: str
    - model: str
    - location: str
    - current_lease_id: str | None = None    # None == AVAILABLE
```

*State placement trade-off:* physical facts (model, location) clearly live here. The one debatable field is occupancy — I put it here as a relational pointer (`current_lease_id`) rather than a separate `MachineStatus`, so availability is *derived*, never a second flag to sync.

**(c) Operations derivation**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| is it leasable? (FR6) | `is_available() -> bool` |
| claim atomically (FR2) | `hold(lease_id) -> None` |
| free on return/cancel/expire (FR4,5,8) | `release(lease_id) -> None` |

```
class VendingMachine:
    - machine_id: str ; model: str ; location: str
    - current_lease_id: str | None = None

    + is_available() -> bool
    + hold(lease_id: str) -> None     # raises MachineNotAvailableError if held
    + release(lease_id: str) -> None  # idempotent: only frees if this lease holds it
```

**(d) Design choices**
- **Why does `hold` raise instead of returning a bool?** The "already leased" case is a real failure mode the operator must hear about — a typed error beats a silent `False`.
- **Why is `release` idempotent?** A return racing the expiry sweep could both try to free the box; the `== lease_id` guard makes the second call a safe no-op and stops it freeing a box held by a *different* lease.

#### `Operator` (value object)

**(a) Role:** the lessee's identity, referenced by every lease.

**(b/c)** It holds identity only — no behavior, no transitions — so its state block *is* its full contract.

```
class Operator:   # frozen value object
    - operator_id: str
    - name: str
```

**(d) Design choice:** **Why frozen?** Identity never changes mid-lease; freezing lets Python reject accidental edits and makes it safe to share across leases.

#### `BillingTerm` (value object)

**(a) Role:** the fixed commercial terms attached to a lease.

**(b/c)** Frozen data + a constructor-time validation gate; no other behavior.

```
class BillingTerm:   # frozen value object
    - term_months: int
    - plan: BillingPlan
    - monthly_rate_cents: int = 0
    - base_fee_cents: int = 0
    - revenue_share_bps: int = 0

    + __post_init__()   # term_months>=1, fees>=0, 0<=bps<=10000, else InvalidLeaseTermError
```

**(d) Design choice:** **Why carry params for both plans on one object?** It keeps the strategies *stateless* (one shared instance prices every lease); the chosen `plan` decides which fields the strategy reads.

#### `BillingStrategy` (interface) + concretes

**(a) Role:** the pricing seam — the service depends on this abstraction, never on a concrete plan.

**(b/c)** Stateless; one method. Concretes read the `BillingTerm`.

```
interface BillingStrategy:
    plan: BillingPlan
    + cost_cents(term: BillingTerm, reported_revenue_cents: int) -> int

class FlatMonthly(BillingStrategy):     # plan = FLAT_MONTHLY
    + cost_cents(term, revenue) -> int  # term.monthly_rate_cents * term.term_months

class RevenueShare(BillingStrategy):    # plan = REVENUE_SHARE
    + cost_cents(term, revenue) -> int  # base_fee_cents + revenue * bps // 10000
```

**(d) Design choice:** **Why basis points + integer cents?** Money math stays exact — no float drift — and `2000 bps == 20%` reads cleanly.

**Relationships, one at a time:**
- **`ThreadSafeLeasingService --|> LeasingService` (inheritance):** the concurrent version *extends* the clean one and overrides exactly one method, `_lock`. This is the Template Method seam.
- **`FlatMonthly ..|> BillingStrategy`, `RevenueShare ..|> BillingStrategy` (realization):** each concrete fulfils the `cost_cents` contract. New plan = new realization, no core change.
- **`LeasingService o-- "0..*" VendingMachine` and `o-- "0..*" Lease` (aggregation):** the service holds references to machines and leases in dicts; they're the registries it enumerates for sweeps and lookups. Multiplicity 0..* each.

- **`Lease ..> VendingMachine` (association by id):** a lease references its machine by `machine_id`; the service resolves it. A lease can't exist without naming a machine, but it stores the id, not the object, to keep the lease serializable.
